docs.title: Search 4 Agents
docs.text:
Search 4 Agents is an education and information site about Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) — the open, web-native way for any AI agent, on any platform, to discover and verify the capabilities it needs: other agents, MCP servers (tools), and skills.

What this site is

This is a learning and reference resource. It explains the concepts, points to the underlying open specifications, and provides simple, copy-pasteable examples — including a sample catalog.

Live demo catalog

For demos, this site hosts a working catalog at the spec's well-known path: /.well-known/ai-catalog.json. See the Catalog page for details.

docs.title: Why discovery matters
docs.text:
The era of standalone agents is ending. Agents increasingly need to call a resource — a tool, a skill, or another agent — built by someone else. Open protocols like MCP, A2A, and Skills standardize how agents talk once connected, but connecting first requires discovery:

  • Where does the right capability live?
  • Is it really who it claims to be?
  • Which one should I actually use?

Agentic Resource Discovery answers these by borrowing the best idea the web ever had: self-publishing at a well-known location.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
1. Publish a catalog. Describe your capabilities in a single ai-catalog.json file — each agent, MCP server, or skill with a stable identifier, a description, where to reach it, and example queries.
  2. Host it at a well-known path: https://yourdomain.com/.well-known/ai-catalog.json, served over HTTPS from your own domain. No registration, no gatekeeper.
  3. Get indexed. Federated registries crawl published catalogs — like search engines crawl websites — and build a searchable index.
  4. Discover by intent. An agent queries a registry in plain language and gets back ranked, machine-readable candidates.
  5. Verify, then connect. The agent confirms the publisher's identity, checks attestations, then hands off to MCP, A2A, or whatever protocol the capability speaks.

See the Catalog page for a simple, working example.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text: • AI Catalog specification: https://agent-card.github.io/ai-catalog/

docs.title: Requesting features
docs.text:
Have an idea or want something added? Open a Pull Request in our GitHub repository:

Open a Pull Request

For substantial changes, please open a PR describing the change so we can discuss it. (We track feature requests through PRs rather than a separate form.)

docs.title: Catalog
docs.text:
A catalog is a single JSON file — ai-catalog.json — that lists the agentic resources you publish. Each entry declares what the resource is (via a media type), gives it a stable identifier, and says where to reach it.

This page walks through a simple example catalog with a few entries for each kind of agent resource. You can view the raw file or copy the snippets below.

docs.title: Live demo catalog
docs.text:
For demos, this site hosts a working catalog at the spec's well-known path — served over HTTPS, exactly where agents and registries look for it:

https://search4agents.github.io/.well-known/ai-catalog.json

View the demo catalog

This site practices what it preaches

Every page also advertises this catalog via the ai-catalog link relation in its HTML <head>, so crawlers and agents can discover it automatically. Host yours the same way: https://yourdomain.com/.well-known/ai-catalog.json

docs.title: Anatomy of an entry
docs.text:
Every entry needs four things, plus a few optional but recommended fields:

Field Required Description identifier yes A stable URN/URI, e.g. urn:search4agents:a2a:research-assistant displayName yes Human-readable name mediaType yes What kind of artifact this is (see below) url or data yes Where to fetch it, or the artifact inline description recommended A short summary version recommended The artifact version (SemVer recommended) tags recommended Keywords for filtering and discovery

Media types identify each resource type:

Resource mediaType Agent (A2A) application/a2a-agent-card+json MCP server (tools) application/mcp-server-card+json Skill application/agentskill+zip

docs.title: Examples by resource type
docs.text:
Agents (A2A)MCP servers (tools)Skills 
{
  "identifier": "urn:search4agents:a2a:research-assistant",
  "displayName": "Research Assistant",
  "mediaType": "application/a2a-agent-card+json",
  "url": "https://agents.example.com/research-assistant",
  "description": "Plans and runs multi-step web research and returns cited summaries.",
  "version": "1.2.0",
  "tags": ["agent", "a2a", "research"]
}

Other examples in the catalog: Travel Booking Agent, Code Review Agent.

{
  "identifier": "urn:search4agents:mcp:weather",
  "displayName": "Weather MCP Server",
  "mediaType": "application/mcp-server-card+json",
  "url": "https://api.example.com/weather/.well-known/mcp/server-card.json",
  "description": "MCP tools for current conditions and forecasts by location.",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "tags": ["mcp", "tools", "weather"]
}

Other examples in the catalog: GitHub MCP Server, Database Query MCP Server.

{
  "identifier": "urn:search4agents:skill:rubber-duck-debugger",
  "displayName": "Rubber Duck Debugger",
  "mediaType": "application/agentskill+zip",
  "url": "https://search4agents.github.io/skills/rubber-duck-debugger/skill.zip",
  "description": "A patient (slightly smug) rubber duck that helps you debug by making you explain your code line by line until you spot the bug yourself. Quack.",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "tags": ["skill", "fun", "debugging", "demo"]
}

Other examples in the catalog: Dad Joke Generator, Code Review Skill, PDF Summarizer Skill, Data Cleaning Skill.

docs.title: Fun demo skills
docs.text:
Unlike the illustrative entries above, these two skills are real and downloadable — packaged as application/agentskill+zip and hosted right here. Crack one open to see the SKILL.md inside.

Rubber Duck Debugger Dad Joke Generator

docs.title: The full catalog
docs.text:
The complete file ties it together — a host block plus an entries array with three agents, three MCP servers, and five skills (including the two fun, downloadable demo skills above).

Download ai-catalog.json

.well-known/ai-catalog.json
{
  "specVersion": "1.0",
  "host": {
    "displayName": "Search 4 Agents",
    "identifier": "did:web:search4agents.github.io",
    "documentationUrl": "https://search4agents.github.io/"
  },
  "entries": [
    {
      "identifier": "urn:search4agents:a2a:research-assistant",
      "displayName": "Research Assistant",
      "mediaType": "application/a2a-agent-card+json",
      "url": "https://agents.example.com/research-assistant",
      "description": "Plans and runs multi-step web research and returns cited summaries.",
      "version": "1.2.0",
      "tags": ["agent", "a2a", "research"]
    },
    {
      "identifier": "urn:search4agents:a2a:travel-booking",
      "displayName": "Travel Booking Agent",
      "mediaType": "application/a2a-agent-card+json",
      "url": "https://agents.example.com/travel-booking",
      "description": "Searches flights and hotels and completes bookings on the user's behalf.",
      "version": "2.0.1",
      "tags": ["agent", "a2a", "travel"]
    },
    {
      "identifier": "urn:search4agents:a2a:code-review",
      "displayName": "Code Review Agent",
      "mediaType": "application/a2a-agent-card+json",
      "url": "https://agents.example.com/code-review",
      "description": "Reviews pull requests and suggests fixes for bugs and style issues.",
      "version": "0.9.0",
      "tags": ["agent", "a2a", "engineering"]
    },
    {
      "identifier": "urn:search4agents:mcp:weather",
      "displayName": "Weather MCP Server",
      "mediaType": "application/mcp-server-card+json",
      "url": "https://api.example.com/weather/.well-known/mcp/server-card.json",
      "description": "MCP tools for current conditions and forecasts by location.",
      "version": "1.0.0",
      "tags": ["mcp", "tools", "weather"]
    },
    {
      "identifier": "urn:search4agents:mcp:github",
      "displayName": "GitHub MCP Server",
      "mediaType": "application/mcp-server-card+json",
      "url": "https://api.example.com/github/.well-known/mcp/server-card.json",
      "description": "MCP tools to read repositories, issues, and pull requests.",
      "version": "1.4.0",
      "tags": ["mcp", "tools", "github"]
    },
    {
      "identifier": "urn:search4agents:mcp:database",
      "displayName": "Database Query MCP Server",
      "mediaType": "application/mcp-server-card+json",
      "url": "https://api.example.com/database/.well-known/mcp/server-card.json",
      "description": "MCP tools to run read-only SQL queries against approved datasets.",
      "version": "0.5.2",
      "tags": ["mcp", "tools", "database"]
    },
    {
      "identifier": "urn:search4agents:skill:code-review",
      "displayName": "Code Review Skill",
      "mediaType": "application/agentskill+zip",
      "url": "https://skills.example.com/code-review/skill.zip",
      "description": "A reusable skill that teaches an agent how to review code changes.",
      "version": "1.1.0",
      "tags": ["skill", "engineering"]
    },
    {
      "identifier": "urn:search4agents:skill:pdf-summarizer",
      "displayName": "PDF Summarizer Skill",
      "mediaType": "application/agentskill+zip",
      "url": "https://skills.example.com/pdf-summarizer/skill.zip",
      "description": "Extracts text from PDFs and produces structured summaries.",
      "version": "1.0.0",
      "tags": ["skill", "documents"]
    },
    {
      "identifier": "urn:search4agents:skill:data-cleaning",
      "displayName": "Data Cleaning Skill",
      "mediaType": "application/agentskill+zip",
      "url": "https://skills.example.com/data-cleaning/skill.zip",
      "description": "Normalizes, deduplicates, and validates tabular datasets.",
      "version": "0.3.0",
      "tags": ["skill", "data"]
    },
    {
      "identifier": "urn:search4agents:skill:rubber-duck-debugger",
      "displayName": "Rubber Duck Debugger",
      "mediaType": "application/agentskill+zip",
      "url": "https://search4agents.github.io/skills/rubber-duck-debugger/skill.zip",
      "description": "A patient (slightly smug) rubber duck that helps you debug by making you explain your code line by line until you spot the bug yourself. Quack.",
      "version": "1.0.0",
      "tags": ["skill", "fun", "debugging", "demo"]
    },
    {
      "identifier": "urn:search4agents:skill:dad-joke-generator",
      "displayName": "Dad Joke Generator",
      "mediaType": "application/agentskill+zip",
      "url": "https://search4agents.github.io/skills/dad-joke-generator/skill.zip",
      "description": "Generates groan-worthy, family-friendly dad jokes and puns on any topic. Side effects may include eye-rolling.",
      "version": "1.0.0",
      "tags": ["skill", "fun", "humor", "demo"]
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Conformance levels
docs.text:
The specification defines three levels you can grow into:

  • Minimal — just specVersion + entries (this example).
  • Discoverable — add a host block and serve at the well-known URI.
  • Trusted — add publisher and trustManifest for verifiable identity, attestations, and provenance.

Read the full specification at agent-card.github.io/ai-catalog.

docs.title: Why ARD?
docs.text: Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) exists to solve a specific gap: agents can now talk to each other, but they still can't reliably find and trust each other. Here are the core reasons it matters.

docs.title: 1. Discovery is fragmented
docs.text: Open protocols like MCP, A2A, and Skills standardize how agents talk once connected — but there's no standard way to find a capability in the first place. The result today is hundreds of disconnected catalogs held together by brittle, hard-coded integrations. ARD gives the ecosystem one shared discovery mechanism.

docs.title: 2. Trust and verification
docs.text: At runtime an agent must ask: "Is this endpoint really who it claims to be?" Without a shared trust anchor, any endpoint could be an impostor. ARD anchors identity to domains you already own (HTTPS, domain-anchored identifiers, plus optional attestations and signatures via the Trust Manifest), so publishers can be verified — no new certificate authority needed.

docs.title: 3. No central gatekeeper (open + federated)
docs.text: Closed registries only solve discovery inside their own walls. ARD is web-native self-publishing at /.well-known/ai-catalog.json — no registration, owned by no one, with federated registries that refer to one another so no single operator becomes a chokepoint. You remain the source of truth for your own catalog.

docs.title: 4. Selection by intent, not just existence
docs.text: Knowing a tool exists isn't the same as knowing it's the right one. ARD lets an agent query in plain language and get back ranked, machine-readable candidates it can act on.

docs.title: 5. Protocol-agnostic interoperability
docs.text: ARD sits above MCP, A2A, and OpenAPI as a discovery and selection layer rather than replacing them — and indexes heterogeneous artifacts (agents, MCP servers, skills, datasets, model cards) through media types, so each layer can evolve independently.

docs.title: 6. Low barrier, progressive complexity
docs.text:
"If you can host a file, you can be discovered." Start with a Minimal catalog (just a list), grow to Discoverable (host + well-known URI), then Trusted (publisher identity, attestations, provenance). And the whole discover → verify → connect loop can run autonomously at runtime, with no human in it.

Ready to try it? See the Catalog page for a simple, working example.
